Hey guys and welcome to the final installment of Six Figure Emails. We're really setting the best for last year. This is called the Anthropomorphic Activator and it's been behind definitely the most famous or infamous email Copy Hacker's has ever sent which we'll share more on in a bit. But behind the infamy or the famousness, weird how you can't say it both ways, there's actually some really powerful persuasion triggers that plan. We're going to look at those and see how to leverage it and then give you a really solid template for you to get very daring and adventurous with in your own copy. So here's how it's going to go down. We're going to give you a bit of background on the template, how it was really born in one single email send in our 10x landing pages launch a few years back. Why we use it, what worked within it specifically, where to use it within a launch or in a nevergreen funnel, how to use it. We're going to talk about what can be personified. You'll see what that means in just a sec. Questions to extract the content. Two variations of the templates, one for general usage and one for e-commerce card abandonment specifically. And then example tear downs in the video below this one. Cool. Bit of background. This email is in copy hackers lore forever subject line. I'm not okay. And then it starts off, hey, it's your landing page here. I either forgotten redheaded stepchild of your marketing funnel, the thing you send thousands of hard earned expensive clicks to you. It haven't checked in for months to see how I was doing. And it goes on and on. We're actually going to look at this example in full detail in the examples below. But this went out. It got an immediate response from our subscribers. Some people calling it overwritten guff. But in total when all is said and done, it got a 4x reply rate, 40 responses. Typically, we'd usually only get 10 on a launch email. So this actually got 40 people taking the time to write back. And it divided our audience. There were about half of them who were sheer advocates and thought it was the most genius thing to ever hit their inbox. And then we had many more adversaries like the person before who broke my heart with overwritten guff. But what it also did was it positioned copy hackers as a daring irreverent voice in online marketing and just showing a willingness to push those boundaries. And yes, it did generate a lot of sales. It was one of the heavy hitters in the launch sequence for 10x landing pages when it first launched, which all kind of begs the question, what is going on here? What did this cheeky little email? Why did this cheeky little email cause such a stir? And of course, how can we isolate, identify and leverage exactly what happened and use it in future launches? But first, what does anthropomorphism personification even mean? I had to say that like 10 times fast before finally getting it down. But anthropomorphic, while the cat in the hat is one example, this guy from many people's childhoods is another. This is Arthur and apparently he's an art bark. Probably. I didn't really know that. But now I do. And all these people, these are all anthropomorphized animals. Mickey Mouse is a mouse that we have given human qualities and human aspects. And that's what anthropomorphism is. It is attributing human characteristics to animals, things, places, people, just essentially humanizing pretty much anything. These are emotions and feelings for one of the biggest movies of all time. What was it called? Inside out. I think it was Disney or Pixar or Disney Pixar. I don't know if they're affiliated now. But yeah, this one is like angry and sad and scared and all these feelings turned into anthropomorphized people, essentially. This movie, a little less well known collateral beauty in 2016, was supposed to be a big blockbuster, still made about $80 million worldwide on a $40 million budget. So hard to call that a failure. But this was with Will Smith. The script initially sold for $2 million because the concept was so unique. And essentially the main characters play anthropomorphized states of fear and loss and death and time, I think. Some combination of those, they personified death and time, things that you just wouldn't give human characteristics to. This movie did it and just based on concept alone, it was one of the highest script sales ever and still grossed $80 million worldwide. So this is everywhere. Animals and things have been selling us forever. This one is from my own childhood. It was a PSA that would play on Sesame Street. This anthropomorphized fish who was in this river and that pipe was connected to this kid's bathroom. And every time he left the faucet running while brushing his teeth, the river would slowly drain and this fish would suffocate and essentially die. And I put a link there in case anyone wants to revisit their childhood horrors. But you really don't have to. But it sold me. I was an impressionable young kid and this fish convinced me to stop wasting water. So it sold me on that. My mom, when she wants me to come over for dinner, she lives about half an hour away. She'll send me this picture of my dog anthropomorphized. Hey, Raya, I really miss you want to come over for dinner. She doesn't send me the invite. She gets the dog to do the dirty work with a rose in his mouth. So this one converts that about 100%. And with all this repressed childhood guilt and trauma out of the way, why are we using? Why are we using it? What is actually going on with all these anthropomorphized film characters and marketing PSA mascots? What is actually happening? Well, here's what's at play, especially for us during a main launch. Having an anthropomorphic email changes the pace during an intensive launch period. It gives our audience a short reprieve from you as the author during that launch. It's an extremely powerful pattern interrupt. It's unexpected. It's unusual and those things tend to grab and hold our attentions sometimes long enough to actually fully see the problems and stakes from a new point of view. You know, they've already seen it from our point of view in telling them what the problems and the stakes are. Now they get to experience it from a new POV from that of the anthropomorphized object. And sometimes that's what we need. Sometimes that's what we need. Sometimes we need to see how the consequences of our actions are or in actions affect others before we finally change. So this allows us to call out our prospect, be a little bit more daring in our tone, a little more confrontational through a proxy, the anthropomorphized object, which is less direct and confronting because it's not us actually doing it. We're getting an anthropomorphized object to do our dirty work for us. But yeah, it gets them to see it from another point of view and see how their actions or inactions extend beyond them. So this is kind of like a smoker who after years of struggling finally quits when his four year old says, I want you to walk me down the aisle. One of those emotional messages like that. And then it triggers our empathetic savior chops. You know, we all have this Superman syndrome, this intrinsic human need to defend the defenseless. You know, how many of us watched Toy Story and wanted to jump into the screen? The second Woody was being driven away in that car to that, you know, awful child. So how many of us want to jump there and save him and bring him back to Andy? Was it? I don't know, whatever it was, bring him back home. But yeah, we see Woody, this anthropomorphic toy that is totally defenseless. And it just triggers our wanting this to save him because we have the capacity and ability to do it, whereas he doesn't. So let's take a deeper look. What we're really coaching here is intimacy with the stakes and outcomes, which are made more intimate by animating the thing, you know, the thing that is, you know, going to suffer or enjoy pleasure based off of the decision that our prospect makes, responsibility that we're more responsible for more than just ourselves, we're responsible for the anthropomorphic object urgency as a result of becoming more intimately aware of those consequences and stakes of our actions or inactions. And of course, we're coaching them the idea that they're actually empowered to act, especially in relation to the proxy. We start to appreciate our ability to act and then for those who can't do it for themselves. So where to use it? We would typically want to send this in the final 24 hours of a launch or funnel because at this point, your audience has already seen the offer and major sales arguments from you from a more straightforward angle, if you will. And therefore the shift in point of view and the unique lens of the anthropomorphic object will have a greater impact for physical goods and e-commerce. This template also works as a really good cart abandonment. And we're going to show you a template on how to use that a little later. So how to use it? Let's turn this weird, crazy topic into something actually actionable that is coming from the cool, but how do I do this marketer? Maybe that's you. It used to be me. So what can be personified? Pretty much anything. The list is endless, but this should give you a starting point. Things, feelings like inside out objects, your car could be anthropomorphized. I'm resting prematurely your house. I'm vulnerable to break ins animals as we saw in all those movies. Your product itself could be anthropomorphized. Their pain could be anthropomorphized. Their hard drive for an antivirus software, a future outcome, you know, or the thing that experiences it. Financial independence can be anthropomorphized. The problem or pain your product helps solve, like your love handles or your menstrual cramp or whatever it is. Anything could be anthropomorphized. A few more examples here. Your start, your confidence, your future partner, your future self. All these things can be used. What you want to do to make sure you're using the right one is ask this first question. What thing experiences the pain, pleasure, gain, loss associated with the buying decision? So if you don't buy home insurance or you do buy home insurance, then it's really your home and the valuable stuff inside it. Maybe the grand piano, for example, that could be anthropomorphized. Of those things, which would your audience be most sympathetic towards? Which is least able to fend for itself? So this is how to also narrow down what objects might elicit the most sympathy. Which one really doesn't stand a chance unless your prospect steps in and defends it through taking action? In what ways is it affected? How do those pain points relate back to your prospect? Because ultimately they share the same pain points. How has it felt neglected and appreciated under serve? What band-aid solutions can the thing call your prospect out on? You'll see how we did that in the Tenex landing pages email. What would life be like for both the thing and your prospect of the problem was fixed? So this is where they actually get to share and celebrate the benefits together, which is always more fun than enjoying the benefits alone. So a lot of things you could do with this one, these questions will certainly get you thinking in the right direction. So as I said, there are two templates you could choose from. The first one is as a closing sales email, you're going to come right off the bat and introduce yourself as the thing. You know, hey, it's your landing page here. Hey, it's your home here. Hey, it's your grand piano, whatever it is. To eliminate confusion, you want to come right out of the gates and introduce yourself as the thing. Drastic times call for drastic measures. Say why you're emailing them and then and agitate the pain in a hyper specific way. So like we are at risk here of, you know, whatever that problem is. Dramatize the pains you've been experiencing. So the anthropomorphic object has been experiencing that pain. Here's where they get to be a little dramatic about it. And then gently or crudely call them out on their inaction or band-aid solutions. Like say, I'm suffering here. You know, I'm not protected. I'm vulnerable. I'm at risk and you've just been pretending that it's not an issue. So this is where you could be confrontational without taking the flack as the marketer because it's the landing page calling them out. Unless the benefits or outcomes you could be experiencing together and really make the close about making things right between your prospect and the object. This email, you're really keeping that frame for as long as possible, even into that call to action and then give the urgency and the stakes and you could end with another call to action. So that is the template for, you know, a general closing sales email as a card abandonment. It's a bit simpler and shorter. Just introduce yourself as a thing. I call the stress like, I'm stuck here on this in this weird, dark cart. I don't know where I belong. I'm kind of in between worlds and purgatory, whatever that looks like for you. And then say, what are you really meant for? You know, he's not, you're not meant to be sitting in a cart. You're meant to be offering certain benefits. What are those? What's the biggest pain or challenge you help them overcome? Like what are you meant to be doing in the world? What is the life purpose of this anthropomorphized physical object or good sitting in a cart? Cause it's not meant to be sitting in a cart. And then agitate your pain as a thing, call to action. And then an optional bribe and e-commerce, whether that's a discount code, whatever that looks like, and then close with another call to action. Cool. So that is the anthropomorphic, anthropomorphic activator template. In the next video, we're going to take a look at two examples. One from 10x landing pages, the infamous, Hades, your landing page email. As well as an example of something you could use for an e-commerce cart abandonment scenario. So catch me in that next video.
